outcome ("restarting" — the new binary is already verified and swapped
in place; the process re-execs right after this response) and
`target_version` the GitHub release tag being installed. The endpoint
fires only on the owner's explicit click; the OPT-IN `updater_auto_update`
setting runs the same verified body unattended in the background.
UpgradeResultDTO.title: UpgradeResultDTO

VersionDTO.description:
Build identity: app version, git sha, and the MCP catalog hash (M1 §3.9).

`version` is the single human-facing version identity: an OFFICIAL package
(bin/release) is stamped with its GitHub Release tag; a self-build keeps the
honest "0.0.0" (the UI then falls back to git_sha + git_time as the build
label).

`git_time` is the commit time of the running `git_sha` (strict ISO-8601), or
None when unavailable (release tarball / no git).

`update_available` drives the software-update card: the server periodically
asks GitHub Releases (repo pkyosx/OffiCraft, anonymous) for the newest
published release (cached, refreshed in the background — unreachable GitHub
NEVER slows this probe) and reports honestly: True iff the newest admissible
release tag is STRICTLY NEWER than the running `version` under semver
ordering (`latest_version` then carries the tag); running >= latest reads
False, and a version label that does not parse as semver on either side
reads False (with a server-side log warning) — never a prompt. A
self-build's "0.0.0" sorts below any published release and therefore
still prompts. Prereleases are admitted only when the
`updater_receive_beta` setting is on.
VersionDTO.title: VersionDTO

WebhookCreateDTO.description: Create one webhook endpoint on a member (M4 §1). `endpoint_id` is REQUIRED, immutable after creation, per-member-unique, and restricted to letters/digits/`_`/`-` (422 otherwise; 409 on a duplicate id). `purpose` is optional editable free text. The server mints the opaque `token` and returns it once on the WebhookEndpointDTO.

platform.description: Verification preset for the public `/in` inlet. Omit or `generic` = current behaviour (URL token only). `slack`/`github` = the server applies that platform's preset: the Slack url_verification challenge handshake and X-Slack-Signature HMAC, or the GitHub X-Hub-Signature-256 HMAC. Immutable after creation.
platform.title: Platform

signing_secret.description: Write-only shared secret for HMAC verification (accepted on create, NEVER echoed back). Slack: the Signing Secret from the app's Basic Information page. GitHub: the secret set when creating the webhook. REQUIRED when `platform` is `slack` or `github`; ignored for `generic`.
signing_secret.title: Signing Secret

WebhookCreateDTO.title: WebhookCreateDTO

WebhookEndpointDTO.description: API representation of one webhook_endpoint (M4 回呼端點, §1). Carries the durable fields the member-detail panel renders. The `token` is the opaque secret the public `/in` inlet identifies (member · endpoint · purpose) by; it rides THIS authenticated owner-facing wire ONLY so the panel can compose + copy the callback URL (the UI masks it visually; copy yields the full URL). It is NEVER on any public or agent-facing wire. `endpoint_id` is the user-chosen, per-member-unique, immutable address key; `purpose` is editable free text; `status` is the enabled/disabled revocation toggle.

delivered_count.description: How many `/in` payloads passed verification AND were delivered to the member as a chat event. Owner-facing observability only — the public `/in` response never reflects any counter.
delivered_count.title: Delivered Count

dropped_count.description: How many `/in` calls that resolved to this token were silently discarded (failed verification, endpoint disabled, member gone). Calls with an unknown token have no endpoint to count against and are excluded by construction.
dropped_count.title: Dropped Count

has_signing_secret.description: Whether this endpoint has a signing secret configured. The secret itself is NEVER echoed on any wire — not even masked; only this boolean is exposed (stricter than the `token` field, which the owner-facing panel still receives).
has_signing_secret.title: Has Signing Secret

last_drop_reason.description: Coarse classification of the most recent silent drop: `sig_failed` (Slack/GitHub signature or timestamp verification failed), `disabled` (endpoint was disabled), or `member_gone` (the bound member no longer resolves). Empty string when nothing was ever dropped.
last_drop_reason.title: Last Drop Reason

last_received_ts.description: Epoch seconds of the LAST `/in` request that resolved to this token — delivered, dropped, or challenge/ping alike. 0.0 when the endpoint has never been called.
last_received_ts.title: Last Received Ts

platform.description: The endpoint's verification preset (`generic`, `slack`, or `github`), fixed at creation.
platform.title: Platform

WebhookEndpointDTO.title: WebhookEndpointDTO

WebhookRequestLogDTO.description: One row of a webhook endpoint's /in debug ring buffer (GET /api/members/{member_id}/webhooks/{endpoint_id}/requests, newest first, at most 5 rows kept per endpoint). Records EVERY request /in resolved to the endpoint's token, whatever the outcome: `delivered` (verified payload landed as a chat), `dropped:sig_failed` / `dropped:disabled` / `dropped:member_gone` (silent drops with their coarse reason), `challenge` (the Slack url_verification handshake), `ping` (a verified GitHub ping). An unknown token has no endpoint to log against, by construction. `headers` is the JSON-serialised request header map (truncated at 4 KiB); `body` is the raw payload text (truncated at 16 KiB); `truncated` marks that either was cut. Owner-only debug wire (requires=owner) - raw external payloads never ride any public or agent-facing surface, and the public /in response stays byte-identical regardless of logging.
WebhookRequestLogDTO.title: WebhookRequestLogDTO

WebhookUpdateDTO.description: Partial edit of a webhook endpoint (M4 §1). PATCH semantics — only supplied fields change. `status` flips the enabled/disabled revocation toggle (a value outside the set is a 422); `purpose` edits the description. `endpoint_id` is immutable and is NOT editable here.

signing_secret.description: Write-only. Supply to set or rotate the endpoint's signing secret (never echoed back). `platform` is immutable and cannot be changed here.
signing_secret.title: Signing Secret

WebhookUpdateDTO.title: WebhookUpdateDTO

WorkerBootContextDTO.description: The outsource worker's boot-context PREVIEW (GET /api/outsource-workers/{id}/boot-context, T-ba6b) — the worker twin of the member panel's /api/bootstrap preview. The server re-runs the SAME buildWorkerBootContext fold the spawn path uses (worker_context.md seed + identity + the bound task in full + the type manual), from the CURRENT DB rows. HONEST: this is what the boot context would look like NOW — not a verbatim record of the spawn-time text (the task/manual may have changed since; nothing is stored). Never carries a worker token.
WorkerBootContextDTO.title: WorkerBootContextDTO

patch.description:
Set an account's display name (AMD step1). PATCH semantics — upserts the
display-name overlay keyed by the STABLE account tag (``account_id``); the id
itself never changes, so every cost SUM / roster attribution the monitoring
fold keys on is untouched. A blank ``display_name`` is a 422. An account tag is
a free telemetry string with no roster row, so ANY non-empty id is aliasable
(the fold shows it once telemetry reports that tag).
patch.summary: Set an account's display name (id = stable tag). Blank name → 422.

get.description:
Stream the committed prebuilt ``ocagent`` binary as a download
(``GET /api/agent/binary`` — ``application/octet-stream``, filename ``ocagent``).

Sibling of ``handle_warden_binary``: the authoritative source that hosts the
ocagent binary so a warden can pull a fresh ocagent to self-update, without a
Go toolchain or a manual build on the agent machine.

Auth: PUBLIC. Like ``handle_warden_binary``, the binary carries NO secret, so the
download is deliberately unauthenticated — a warden can pull a fresh ocagent with
a bare ``curl`` and no Authorization header. Making it public (vs. GATED) is a low
risk given a secret-free artifact.

503 if the binary is absent (server started without a built ``bin/ocagent``) —
an honest, actionable failure rather than a 404 that reads like a bad route.
get.summary: Download the prebuilt ocagent binary (octet-stream) for an agent.

post.description:
Ingest one agent's context-window gauge (statusLine → server; §3.4 #18).
Stored purely IN-MEMORY on ``app.state.context_gauge`` (no DB, ephemeral — a
fresh gauge on restart is fail-safe). A non-numeric ``context_pct`` is a flat 400
(NOT a 422). Fans a ``context`` signal so the monitoring surface refetches.

IDENTITY FROM TOKEN: the gauge key is the CALLER (``current_actor`` — the verified
JWT ``sub``), NEVER a self-reported ``agent_id`` (that param was removed) — so an
agent can only ever report its OWN context, structurally.
post.summary: Ingest an agent's context gauge (in-memory; bad body → 400).

post.description:
Change the owner password (owner-gated). `current_password` is re-verified
against the stored argon2id hash (401 on a mismatch — a stolen live session
cannot silently rotate the credential). On success the new hash is stored,
`auth.password_changed_at` is stamped (owner tokens with iat < changed_at are
refused from then on — agent/warden tokens unaffected), and the response
carries a fresh owner token so the current session survives its own change.
post.summary: Change the owner password (verifies the current one).

post.description:
First-time owner-password claim (PUBLIC, claim-token gated). The server mints
a one-shot claim token at first boot (no password set) and prints it ONLY to
the local serve log / installer banner; presenting it here proves host shell
access — the gate that stops a public-tunnel visitor from claiming a fresh
server. Order of checks: password already set → 409 (the token is never
consulted); claim token mismatch → 401; otherwise the argon2id hash is stored,
the claim token is deleted, and a fresh owner token is minted (the response
logs the caller straight in).
post.summary: First-run: set the owner password (one-shot claim token gate).

get.description:
First-run probe: whether an owner password has been set (PUBLIC — the UI
branches first-run setup vs login on this single bit; nothing else about the
auth state is disclosed).
get.summary: First-run probe: has the owner password been set?

post.description:
Assemble an agent's boot package (§3.4 #29, §2.4).

Resolves the role (explicit ``role`` → the member's ``role_key`` → the default
``assistant``), folds role definition + global context + lessons, and packs
them into a single readable ``context``. When ``member_id`` is supplied (a
warden spawn) the response carries a freshly minted MEMBER JWT
(``scope="agent"``, ``sub=member_id`` — NOT an owner token); a UI preview omits
``member_id`` and gets ``token=None``.

404s: an unknown ``member_id``, or a resolved role that is neither a seed role
nor an owner overlay.
post.summary: Assemble an agent boot context + mint the member JWT (spawn seam).

get.description:
List the owner's chat stream oldest→newest, capped to the most recent
``limit`` (default 30; §3.4 #17). ``?with=<id>`` filters to messages a
participant is in (``sender == id`` OR ``recipient == id``); the limit is
applied AFTER the filter. A NEGATIVE limit (``?limit=-1``) disables the cap
and returns the WHOLE filtered history (any full-history consumer; the M2-3
gallery moved OFF this path to the flattened ``GET /api/chat/attachments``
— see ``handle_list_chat_attachments``).

HISTORY PAGING (scrollback): ``?before_ts=<ts>&before_id=<id>`` — a composite
keyset cursor (both together, else 422) — returns the ``limit`` messages
strictly OLDER than the cursor (``ts < before_ts`` OR ``ts == before_ts AND
id < before_id``; equal-ts collisions tie-break on id, matching the stream's
``ts, id`` order), still oldest→newest. A page shorter than ``limit`` means
the history is exhausted. A HISTORY PAGE NEVER ADVANCES THE READ WATERMARK
— reading old context is not reading the conversation's newest messages, so
the auto read-receipt below fires only on a cursorless list.

AUTO READ-RECEIPT: when a specific conversation is requested (``?with=<peer>``)
WITHOUT a cursor, listing it IS reading it — the caller (``actor``, the
verified JWT ``sub``) has
now seen every returned message, so we advance its read watermark for that
conversation to the newest returned message ts (monotonic upsert; a no-op when
the conversation is empty or nothing newer landed). This is the agent-side
automatic already-read core: an agent that lists a conversation is marked as
having read it, with no extra call.

schema.title: Response Handle List Chat Api Chat Get

get.summary: List the chat stream (?with=<id>&limit=<n>; oldest→newest).

post.description:
Post one chat message (§3.4 #16). The sender is ALWAYS the VERIFIED JWT
``sub`` (``current_actor``) — never the body's ``from`` (ignored), so a sender
can not be forged. The server mints the ``id`` (``c-<hex>``) and timestamps.
The write funnels through the Repository, which auto-fans one ``chat`` SSE
delta (§3.7).

A message may carry text and/or MULTIPLE attachments via the generic
``attachments`` list (each ``{data_b64, filename?, mime?}``; image is the
special case where the mime is ``image/*``). The count is capped
(``_CHAT_ATTACHMENTS_MAX_COUNT``); each item is decoded, mime/size-validated,
and stored as a SEPARATE blob (via the Repository). The message carries only
the light refs ``meta["attachments"] = [{id, mime, filename}]`` so the DTO
needs no blob read. ALL blobs are decoded/validated BEFORE any is stored, so
a rejected item never leaves earlier siblings orphaned as dangling blobs of a
message that was never posted. A truly empty message (no text AND no
attachments) is rejected 400.

An attachments item may alternatively be a light REFERENCE ``{id}`` to a
blob already in the store (uploaded via ``POST /api/chat/attachments``);
refs and inline items mix freely under the same count cap, and validation
stays all-or-nothing (every ref is resolved AND every inline item is
decoded before any new blob is stored). The stored blob's mime/filename
are authoritative on a ref (an accompanying ``filename``/``mime`` is
ignored); an unknown ``id`` is a 400; ``id`` together with ``data_b64`` is
a 400. The SAME blob may end up referenced by more than one message (or by
a reply card); the member-purge cascade deletes a blob only when no
surviving record references it.
post.summary: Post a chat message (sender = verified JWT sub; auto SSE fan-out).

get.description: Serve a chat attachment blob (``GET /api/chat/attachment/``). GATED and owner-scoped exactly like ``handle_list_chat``: the ``owner`` dep re-verifies the token (deny-by-default) and fixes the single data scope, and the lookup is scoped to that owner — a blob outside the owner's scope (or nonexistent) is a 404, so a caller can not fetch another owner's attachment. Returns the raw bytes with the stored ``mime`` as the media type. DISPOSITION SPLIT (M2-3 gallery「開新分頁預覽」 vs 「下載」): an IMAGE is served with no disposition at all (unchanged — ```` keeps working); any other PREVIEWABLE mime (text/*, application/pdf — see ``_is_previewable_mime``) is served ``inline; filename=""`` so a new tab RENDERS it instead of force-downloading; everything else keeps ``attachment; filename=""`` and downloads under its original name. SECURITY: an inline ``text/html`` blob would otherwise execute attacker-supplied script ON THIS ORIGIN (an agent-uploaded page could read the owner JWT out of localStorage). Every non-image inline preview therefore carries ``Content-Security-Policy: sandbox`` — the document still renders visually, but script execution and same-origin reach are disabled. SHARE-SIG (third credential, this route ONLY — precedence Authorization header → ``?token=`` → ``?sig=``): when the request carries NO bearer credential at all, a ``?sig=`` query param is accepted instead — an HMAC-SHA256 over exactly this ``attachment_id`` under a domain-separated key derived from the server signing secret (minted by ``GET /api/chat/attachments/{attachment_id}/share-link``). A valid sig authorizes reading THIS ONE blob and nothing else; a bad/foreign sig is 401; ``?sig=`` on any other route is ignored (401 missing credentials). Like ``?token=``, the param is an auth credential OUTSIDE the OpenAPI parameter schema. Permanent by design (no expiry/revocation — beta).
get.summary: Serve a chat attachment blob (owner-gated; raw bytes + stored mime).

get.description:
List EVERY attachment of a member's conversations, flattened
(``GET /api/chat/attachments?with=<member_id>``) — the gallery query.

MEMBER-PERSPECTIVE scope (Seth M2 acceptance batch 16): every message the
member PARTICIPATES in (``sender == with_`` OR ``recipient == with_``) —
owner↔member in BOTH directions AND the member's inter-agent conversations
(member↔other agent, both directions). A conversation the member is not part
of (e.g. owner↔another member) never leaks in.

Each row is one attachment ref plus its message's SENDER IDENTITY: ``from``
(the verified sender id) and ``from_name`` (the display name resolved from
the owner's roster, ANY status — a dismissed sender still reads by name;
``""`` for the owner / an unresolvable id, honest empty). Rows come
newest→oldest (stable — a multi-attachment message keeps its posted
attachment order). Derived entirely from the light ``meta["attachments"]``
refs — no blob read.

READ-ONLY: unlike ``handle_list_chat``'s ``?with=`` path this endpoint does
NOT advance any read watermark — opening the gallery is not reading the
thread. A blank ``with`` is a 422 (the gallery is always per-member).

schema.title: Response Handle List Chat Attachments Api Chat Attachments Get

get.summary: List every attachment of a member's conversations (?with=<member_id>; flattened, sender-labelled, newest→oldest).

post.description:
Upload ONE attachment blob into the shared chat attachment store
(``POST /api/chat/attachments`` — the SEND-side streaming seam; the receive
side is ``GET /api/chat/attachment/{attachment_id}``). The raw request body
IS the file bytes (``application/octet-stream``; NOT base64, NOT
multipart), so a large file streams server-side instead of ballooning 4/3×
inside an LLM tool call — ``ocagent upload <path>`` is the canonical
client. The response is the light ref ``{id, mime, filename}``: paste it
(or just ``{id}``) into ``post_chat``'s ``attachments`` to send it.

``?filename=`` names the blob (optional; an unnamed image defaults to
``pasted-image.<ext>`` exactly like an inline post — an unnamed non-image
stays unnamed and serves under its id). ``?mime=`` declares the media type
(optional; omitted → magic-byte image sniff, else
``application/octet-stream``). The request ``Content-Type`` header is
deliberately IGNORED: HTTP clients default it to
``application/octet-stream``, indistinguishable from an explicit
declaration — the explicit channel is ``?mime=``.

Size caps are the inline path's exactly (one mechanism, not two): an
``image/*`` blob caps at 20 MB, anything else at 100 MB (413-free: an
over-cap or empty body is a flat 400).

AUTH + SCOPE: gated at the same ``machine`` floor as posting chat. Blobs
in this single-owner instance are instance-global — every authed principal
can already read every blob via the serve route — so referencing is
EXISTENCE-ONLY and no uploader identity is recorded (it would add no
confidentiality). A blob that is uploaded but never referenced simply sits
in the store (no GC — beta); the member-purge cascade removes only blobs
whose every referencing record is gone. Excluded from the MCP tool surface
(a binary ingest seam like the blob GET, not a tool — the catalog and
catalog_hash are unchanged).
post.summary: Upload one attachment blob (raw octet-stream body; returns the light ref).

get.description:
Mint the permanent share link for ONE attachment
(``GET /api/chat/attachments/<id>/share-link``). GATED like every chat
route (any authenticated principal); 404 for an unknown blob id.

Returns ``{url}`` — the attachment's serve path
(``/api/chat/attachment/<id>``) carrying a ``?sig=`` HMAC-SHA256
credential over exactly that attachment id, signed with a
domain-separated key derived from the server signing secret. The sig is
PERMANENT (no expiry, no revocation — owner-accepted beta trade-off)
and grants NOTHING beyond reading this one blob: a different id, a
tampered sig, or any other endpoint stays 401 deny-by-default.
get.summary: Mint a permanent single-file share link (?sig= HMAC; grants read of this one attachment only).

post.description:
Mark a conversation read up to a watermark (``POST /api/chat/mark-read``).

The reader is ALWAYS the VERIFIED JWT ``sub`` (``actor``) — never a
client-supplied field, so a read receipt can not be forged (mirrors chat post's
sender). ``body.peer`` is the OTHER participant; ``body.last_read_ts`` is the
newest message ts the reader has seen. The upsert is MONOTONIC (the watermark
only ever advances), so a stale report never rewinds it. Funnels through the
Repository, which fans one ``chat_read`` SSE delta (§3.7). A blank ``peer`` is a
422 (via the DTO's required field).
post.summary: Mark a conversation read up to a watermark (reader = verified sub).

get.description:
List the owner's chat read receipts (``GET /api/chat/reads``). ``?with=<id>``
filters to receipts for that peer conversation (``peer_id == id``) — the FE
reads this to know whether the OTHER party has read the owner's messages. Each
receipt is a per-conversation last-read watermark.

schema.title: Response Handle List Chat Reads Api Chat Reads Get

get.summary: List chat read receipts (?with=<peer>; per-conversation watermark).

get.description: The office nav red-dot signal: the caller's TOTAL unread chat messages across every peer. A dot shows when > 0. Kept as its own endpoint so the dot can refetch cheaply on ``chat`` / ``chat_read`` SSE deltas without pulling the roster.
get.summary: Total chat unread count (the office nav red dot).

get.description:
Owner-scoped SSE delta stream (M1 §3.4 #6, §3.7).

GATED — an unauthenticated request is denied 401 at the gate (the ``owner``
dep re-verifies, so a direct call still fails closed). A valid caller gets a
live ``text/event-stream`` fed by the app's `SSEHub`: every fenced write
(the Repository commit funnel) fans ONE delta out to this owner's listeners.
The client contract is reconcile-by-refetch (§3.7): a delta signals "this
topic changed → refetch", it is not merged in place. A `: heartbeat` comment
keeps the connection warm; reconnect/replay is the client's concern.
get.summary: SSE delta stream (owner-scoped fan-out; reconcile-by-refetch).

get.description:
Read the owner's 使用者自訂 (user-custom) additive block (§3.4 #20). Empty
(never written / reset) → ``text=""``/``is_default=True``. The read-only
system-interaction + boot-sequence seed blocks are NOT served here — they have
no owner-editable representation at all.
get.summary: Read the user-custom additive context block (empty = is_default).

post.description:
Whole-block replace of the user-custom additive block (§3.4 #21). Writes
ONLY the 使用者自訂 block (``is_default`` → False) and fans a delta — the
system-interaction / boot-sequence seeds are untouchable by construction.
post.summary: Whole-block replace of the user-custom additive block ({text}).

post.description:
Reset the user-custom block to empty (§3.4 #22): an idempotent TOMBSTONE so
the folded read falls back to the empty default (``is_default`` → True) and the
assembled boot context skips the block.
post.summary: Reset the user-custom block to empty (idempotent tombstone).

get.description: Liveness probe. Always ok when the server is up.
get.summary: Liveness probe — 200 {"status":"ok"}.

get.description:
Read the folded per-role lessons doc for ``(role_key, task_type)`` (§3.4 #27).
READ is unrestricted: any authenticated identity may read ANY role's lessons.
M1 uses a single fixed task_type (§9.7); all roles fold against the one file seed
until a role's overlay diverges it.
get.summary: Read a per-role lessons doc (per role_key; overlay ⊕ seed).

post.description:
Whole-doc replace of a PER-ROLE lessons doc (§3.4 #28).

Per-role WRITE authz (load-bearing): an ``agent``-scoped caller may write ONLY
its OWN member's ``role_key`` — the server reads that role_key from the member row
keyed by the VERIFIED token ``sub`` (``actor``), NEVER from a client field; a
mismatch (or a member with no role) is a flat 403. Any NON-agent scope (the
owner's ``scope="owner"`` token, or a human principal) may write ANY role's
lessons. This replaces the old blanket governance choke: agents can now curate
their own role's learnings, but cannot poison another role's.

Writes the owner overlay (``is_default`` → False) and fans a ``lessons`` delta.
post.summary: Whole-doc replace of a per-role lessons doc ({text}).

post.description:
Anchor-addressed PATCH of a PER-ROLE lessons doc (§3.4 #28b) — the write cost scales with the CHANGE, not the doc (a whole-doc ``replace_lessons`` stops fitting in one model output as the doc grows; keep replace as the last resort).

Semantics: ``edits`` apply IN ORDER against the doc ``get_lessons`` serves (overlay ⊕ seed fold); each non-empty ``old`` must match the current text exactly once (0 hits or >1 hits → flat 400, WHOLE batch rejected, zero writes); an empty ``old`` appends ``new`` at the end. Concurrency is last-write-wins with the unique anchor as a natural optimistic lock: a concurrent write that moved the anchor turns the next patch into a 400, never a silent mis-splice. A patch that empties the doc (or shrinks it below a tenth of its size) is refused unless ``allow_shrink=true`` — the r-76 wipe-guard posture.

Per-role WRITE authz — identical to ``replace_lessons``: an ``agent``-scoped caller may patch ONLY its OWN member's ``role_key`` (read from the roster by the VERIFIED token sub, never a client field); any non-agent scope patches any role.

Writes the owner overlay (``is_default`` → False) and fans a ``lessons`` delta. The receipt carries ``size``/``sha256`` verification anchors over the resulting doc.
post.summary: Patch a per-role lessons doc by unique anchors ({edits:[{old,new}]}).

post.description:
Exchange the owner password for an owner-scoped JWT (M1 §3.5).

The single PUBLIC business entry point. The submitted password is compared
(constant-time) against the configured `[auth].password`; on a match an
HS256 JWT scoped to the single owner (`sub = DEFAULT_OWNER`, `scope=owner`)
is minted with the configured TTL, and is thereafter the one credential for
REST / MCP / SSE. A wrong password — or no password configured at all
(deny-by-default) — is a flat 401, with no hint which it was.

The signing secret and the expected password are resolved once at
`create_app` time and held on `app.state`; this handler never sees a
hard-coded secret.
post.summary: Owner login: exchange the password for an owner-scoped JWT.

get.description:
List the owner's machines for the FE picker/panel (``GET /api/machines``).

One row per ACTIVE ``kind=="warden"`` member. ``machine_id`` is the warden's own
id (the stable machine id an agent binds to via its ``host``); ``display_name``
folds the machine-alias overlay (keyed by that machine id) over the member's name
(same fold the monitoring machines section uses); ``online`` is the live
SSE-connection projection for that warden.

schema.title: Response Handle List Machines Api Machines Get

get.summary: List machines (active wardens): machine_id/display_name/online.

post.description:
Onboard a machine: mint a NEW warden-kind member whose own id IS the machine
id + mint its exec-token (admin-gated on the route table: requires="admin_agent").

THE MACHINE IDENTITY is the warden member's server-minted id (``m-<uuid12>``). The
warden carries NO self-binding (``desired_machine_id`` stays "") — routing resolves
it
by ``get_member``\ ing that machine id (== the warden's own id) directly. This
replaces the old fragile hand-typed ``host`` string: a typo can no longer break
agent wake, because agents bind to this stable minted id (see
``handle_activate_member``). Every onboard creates a DISTINCT machine — there is
NO host-dedup (each call is a new physical machine).

``display_name`` (required, 422 if blank) is the human label; it is written as a
MachineAlias overlay keyed by the machine id (== member.id).

The response carries the member id (== machine_id), the minted exec-token
(``scope="agent"``, ``sub=member_id``, TTL = min(ttl_days*86400,
MAX_AGENT_TTL_SECS)), and the copy-paste ``boot_command`` the operator runs on
that machine to install the warden (identity rides in the token's ``sub``).
post.summary: Onboard a machine: new warden member (id == machine id) + exec-token.

post.description:
Exchange a one-time claim code for the machine's exec-token (``POST /api/machines/claim``).

The onboard / boot-command responses no longer template the machine's long-lived exec-token into the copy-paste one-liner; they mint a short-lived (600 s), SINGLE-USE claim code instead and the served ``install.sh?code=`` script calls this endpoint to redeem it. On a valid, unexpired, unused code the response carries a freshly minted exec-token (``scope="agent"``, ``sub=machine_id``, default 90-day TTL capped at 400 days — the same mint onboard performed) plus ``expires_in`` and the ``machine_id`` the token is bound to. Redemption CONSUMES the code atomically: a second call with the same code — or any invalid/expired code — is a flat 401 with no hint which it was (no guessing oracle).

Auth: PUBLIC — the code IS the credential (possession proves the caller holds a boot command the owner just minted). Codes live in memory only (TTL 600 s); a server restart voids them, which reads as expiry.
post.summary: Exchange a one-time claim code for the machine's exec-token.

patch.description:
Set a machine's display name (AMD step1). PATCH semantics — upserts the
display-name overlay keyed by the STABLE host string (``machine_id``); the id
itself never changes, so the roster host-grouping the monitoring fold keys on
is untouched. A blank ``display_name`` is a 422. A host is a free roster string
with no standalone row, so ANY non-empty id is aliasable.
patch.summary: Set a machine's display name (id = stable host). Blank name → 422.

get.description:
Re-fetch a machine's boot command anytime (``GET
/api/machines/{machine_id}/boot-command``).

The onboard result is shown once; an owner must be able to get the copy-paste
installer for an existing machine again later (Seth: "I can get the machine
boot command anytime") without re-onboarding (which would mint a NEW machine).
Resolves the ACTIVE warden member whose id IS ``machine_id`` (404 otherwise),
RE-MINTS a fresh exec-token (default machine TTL, the same mint onboard uses),
and rebuilds the one-liner via the shared ``_build_boot_command``.

Governance: the SAME ``requires="admin_agent"`` route choke onboard uses. Route
is GATED + ``mcp_exclude`` — it mints a credential (an exec-token), so it is a
mint seam, not an agent tool.
get.summary: Re-fetch a machine's boot command anytime (re-mints its exec-token).

post.description:
Bootstrap on server: install a machine's warden ON THE SERVER HOST in one
click (``POST /api/machines/{machine_id}/bootstrap-here``).

The common case is that the officraft server RUNS ON the machine being
provisioned, so instead of copy-pasting the boot command into a shell the owner
clicks once and the server installs the warden locally. It resolves the ACTIVE
warden member (404 otherwise), re-mints a fresh exec-token, resolves the
ocwarden binary the SAME way ``handle_warden_binary`` does (503 if absent), and
runs ``<ocwarden> install`` as a subprocess.

Governance: OWNER-ONLY via the route table (``requires="owner"``) —
DELIBERATELY stricter than onboard's admin choke. This runs code on
the server host (a privileged local action), so it must be strictly owner-scoped:
not even the mira governance agent may trigger a host install.

SECURITY: the subprocess uses an argv list (``[bin, "install"]``), NEVER a shell
string — zero command-injection surface. The token/base/id ride in ``env`` (a
COPY of ``os.environ`` so PATH/HOME are inherited — the warden installs into the
server user's ~/.officraft, which is the point). The runner is INJECTABLE via
``app.state.bootstrap_runner`` so tests never launch a real installer.

Non-zero exit is NOT raised: the one-warden guard in ocwarden refusing (exit 1)
is a legitimate outcome — ``ok=false`` with the guard message surfaced in ``log``
so the FE shows the reason. A timeout is a clear error, not a hang.
post.summary: Bootstrap on server: install this machine's warden on the host.

post.description:
Teardown on server: tear a machine's warden down ON THE SERVER HOST in one
click (``POST /api/machines/{machine_id}/teardown-here``).

The symmetric inverse of ``handle_bootstrap_here``. The common case is that the
officraft server RUNS ON the machine being torn down, so instead of copy-
pasting the teardown command into a shell the owner clicks once and the server
runs ``<ocwarden> teardown`` locally (``launchctl bootout`` + remove the install
artifacts — the daemon stops because launchd stops it, never pkill). It resolves
the ACTIVE warden member (404 otherwise), resolves the ocwarden binary the SAME
way ``handle_bootstrap_here`` does (503 if absent), and runs the teardown via an
INJECTABLE runner (``app.state.teardown_runner``).

Governance: OWNER-ONLY via the route table (``requires="owner"``) —
the SAME strict choke ``handle_bootstrap_here`` uses, DELIBERATELY stricter than
the admin choke of the remote-command ``handle_uninstall_machine`` /
``handle_delete_machine``. This runs code on the server host (a privileged local
action), so not even the mira governance agent may trigger a host teardown.

SAFETY — CONFIRM-THEN-REMOVE: the warden member is soft-deleted ONLY when the
daemon is CONFIRMED torn down (``exit_code == 0``). ocwarden teardown is
idempotent and CONFIRMS the bootout (it polls ``launchctl print`` until launchd
reports the label truly gone — bootout is async), so 0 means "the launchd job
is CONFIRMED booted out and the artifacts are gone"; a NON-ZERO exit (or a
timeout) means the local teardown did NOT complete or confirm
— in that case the member row is LEFT ON THE ROSTER (``removed=false``) so a
failed local teardown never orphans a still-running daemon from its registry
row. ``log`` (merged stdout+stderr) surfaces the reason so the FE shows it.

SECURITY: the subprocess uses an argv list (``[bin, "teardown"]``), NEVER a
shell string. env is a COPY of ``os.environ`` (HOME/PATH inherited — the warden
was installed into the server user's ~/.officraft, which is what teardown
removes). The runner is INJECTABLE so tests never touch launchctl.
post.summary: Teardown on server: tear this machine's warden down on the host.

delete.description:
Delete a machine (``DELETE /api/machines/{member_id}``, T-IUD) — a PURE
soft-delete of the record, dispatching NO warden command.

The third INDEPENDENT verb (delete ≠ uninstall ≠ stop): DELETE removes the machine
from the ROSTER — it does NOT tear the warden daemon off the box (that is the
``uninstall`` verb, above). It reuses the ``handle_dismiss_member`` soft-delete
(``status="removed"`` + intent ``desired_state="offline"``, then re-put) — NEVER a
hard
delete, so the audit row + the token's ``sub`` attribution survive; no token
revocation infrastructure (HS256 is stateless; the exec-token expires by TTL —
kyle's decree). It hands back NO ``teardown_command`` placeholder: DELETE does not
command removal, so it never returns an installer/uninstaller line (the old
teardown-command occupancy is gone — uninstall is now its own seam).

Admin-gated on the route table (requires="admin_agent"). Kind guard: a
non-warden member is
a 409 (DELETE targets a MACHINE, not an arbitrary member) — the same guard the old
teardown seam used, so this never becomes a back door that soft-deletes any roster
member the choke happens to admit. dismiss (DELETE /api/members) remains the seam
for non-warden members. The always-present server-self machine is REFUSED (409):
row 1 is the host running the server and is never deletable.
delete.summary: Delete a machine: soft-delete its warden record (no command sent).

post.description:
Uninstall a machine (``POST /api/machines/{member_id}/uninstall``, T-IUD).

The MACHINE-lifecycle verb: write the owner INTENT ``desired_state="uninstall"`` so
the
server reconcile arm (``_decide_uninstall``) drives the single ``uninstall`` RPC
down to the warden (which runs ``ocwarden uninstall`` on its box). Sets INTENT
only — the actual removal happens over the NAT SSE seam, and the warden's
command_result receipt then converges the record (fold: ok → ``desired_state`` back
to
OFFLINE, record KEPT re-installable; fail → left ``uninstall`` for retry).

This is DISTINCT from teardown-here (a server-host local subprocess) and from
DELETE (a pure roster soft-delete): uninstall is the REMOTE warden-command path,
the counterpart of ``activate``'s desired_state=online. It KEEPS the machine record
(``status`` stays ACTIVE) — an uninstalled machine is re-installable — so it is
NOT a soft-delete.

ONLINE-vs-OFFLINE: only an ONLINE warden can receive the command (its SSE
downstream must be live). When the warden is ALREADY OFFLINE there is no live
connection to drain the frame, so the machine is treated as ALREADY uninstalled:
``desired_state`` is left OFFLINE (nothing to reconcile) and NO command is promised.
``dispatched`` reports which path was taken.

Governance: the SAME ``requires="admin_agent"`` route choke as onboard / the
remote DELETE. Kind guard: a non-warden member is a 409 (uninstall targets a
MACHINE, not an arbitrary member) — mirroring the old teardown guard so this seam
is not a back door onto any roster member the choke admits.
post.summary: Uninstall a machine: drive the uninstall RPC to its warden.

post.description:
Upgrade a machine's binaries NOW (``POST /api/machines/{member_id}/upgrade``,
T-5f01).

The owner's one-click upgrade for a machine the table shows as ``stale``: the
server enqueues the ``update`` warden-command verb (spec/sse.md §7) onto the
machine's live SSE downstream, and the warden's dispatch kicks its EXISTING
self-update reconcile immediately (the T-c93d kick seam — download +
verify-before-swap + atomic swap + exec-in-place), instead of waiting out the
poll backstop. Fire-and-forget by design: NO desired_state intent is written
and nothing durable changes — the self-update loop is idempotent (content-hash
swap oracle), so a spurious or repeated upgrade is a cheap no-op, and
convergence is observed through the next heartbeat's ``binaries`` fingerprints
flipping ``bin_status`` to ``current``.

ONLINE-vs-OFFLINE: same reachability gate as every warden command — an online
warden gets the frame (``dispatched=true``); an offline warden gets nothing
(``dispatched=false``, and its own reconnect-kick self-updates it the moment
it comes back anyway). An older warden build that predates the ``update`` verb
refuses the frame as unknown-rpc (logged + skipped, reader loop unharmed).

Governance: ``requires="owner"`` — a cockpit-only host-lifecycle action (like
bootstrap-here / teardown-here), never an agent tool. Kind guard: an unknown,
removed, or non-warden member resolves 404 (machine not found).
post.summary: Upgrade a machine: kick its warden's self-update NOW.

post.description:
MCP JSON-RPC transport (M1 §3.4 #7). GATED — the real transport.

GATED (deny-by-default at the gate). Thin: hands the request to
``service.mcp.transport.dispatch``, which parses the JSON-RPC envelope and —
for ``tools/call`` — re-enters the same REST route in-process (so the one auth
gate + DI + validation + handler guards all run unchanged). The import is lazy
to avoid an import cycle (``service.mcp`` reflects the route table).
post.summary: MCP JSON-RPC transport (tools/list + tools/call over the routes).

get.description:
List the owner's roster (§3.4 #8). Soft-removed members are omitted (the
audit row survives). ``online`` is the live SSE-connection projection
(``hub.online_members``) — the single online source, not the durable field.
``machine`` is the OBSERVED position (``observed_host`` — SSE machine claim →
telemetry.machine → desired_machine_id), the SAME source the monitoring surface
reads, so the detail panel shows where a member ACTUALLY runs (not its DESIRED
``desired_machine_id``, which differs after a relocate until reconcile lands).

``unread_count`` (M2-1 red dot, upgraded to a count badge) is the pure
INVERSE of the chat_read watermark, computed for the CALLER (``actor`` = the
verified JWT sub, per the caller-identity convention — never a parameter):
how many messages the member has sent the caller newer than the caller's
last-read watermark for that conversation
(``domain.chat_read.unread_counts``). Reusing the same receipt the read-✓
badge reads means read/unread can never disagree; clearing is the EXISTING
chokes (``handle_list_chat`` auto-mark on entering a conversation +
``POST /api/chat/mark-read``), no parallel mechanism. It is independent of
presence — an offline member can be unread.

schema.title: Response Handle List Members Api Members Get

get.summary: List the owner's roster (presence-derived MemberDTO[]).

post.description:
Hire a roster member (§3.4 #9; pure seam, no UI). The owner assigns the
display ``name``; the server mints the ``id`` (``m-<hex>`` — never client
supplied, it is the attribution key). The member starts offline/active; hiring
does NOT spawn a runtime. A blank ``name`` is a 422.

PRIVILEGE GUARD (closes the hire escalation hole): ``kind`` / ``role_key``
are PRIVILEGE-BEARING fields — ``kind="warden"`` makes the row a MACHINE
principal (a warden-command target) and ``role_key="assistant"`` makes it an
ADMIN principal (governance capability). A plain hire (name/model/effort)
stays open to any authenticated caller (unchanged), but a body carrying a
non-empty ``kind`` or ``role_key`` demands the caller itself resolve to at
least ``admin_agent`` (the same class the onboard / role-write seams
require) — otherwise 403. Before this guard ANY agent token could self-serve
a warden or an admin member.
post.summary: Hire a member (server mints the id). Pure seam, no UI (§9.1).

delete.description:
Dismiss a member (§3.4 #15; pure seam, no UI) — a SOFT delete:
``status="removed"`` + intent ``desired_state="offline"``, then re-put (never a hard
tombstone, so attribution/audit survives). Writes INTENT only — the warden
reads ``desired_state="offline"`` and tears down the live session.

RBAC (control-others): route-table ``requires="admin_agent"`` — only an
owner-scoped token OR an admin-role (assistant) member may dismiss a
member; an ordinary agent → 403.
delete.summary: Dismiss a member (soft delete). Pure seam, no UI (§9.1).

get.description:
Read one roster member (§3.4 #10). 404 if absent or soft-removed. ``machine``
is the OBSERVED position (``observed_host``), same as the list endpoint.
get.summary: Read one roster member (removed → 404).

patch.description:
Partially update a member's owner-editable fields (§3.4 #11: name / model /
effort). PATCH semantics — only supplied fields change. A blank ``name`` is a
422; an ``effort`` outside low/medium/high is a 422. The immutable ``id`` and
every runtime field (presence/desired_state/status) are untouched.
patch.summary: Edit a member (name / model / effort). Blank name / bad effort → 422.

post.description:
Activate a member (§3.4 #12): write the owner's INTENT ``desired_state="online"``
(and bind the reconciling ``host`` when named). Sets intent ONLY — does NOT
flip ``online`` (the ACTUAL state). The server can't reach the host, so the
warden reads ``desired_state``/``host``, spawns, and reports ``online`` back via the
presence endpoint. A ``member`` delta fans out immediately.

ALWAYS FORCE-REVIVE (Seth: "always revive from a wrong state"). Wake NO LONGER
has a winding-down gate — the old ``stopping`` → 409 refusal is GONE. A member
stuck in ANY wrong winding-down state (the survived-stop / SSE-reconnect case:
still ``online`` yet pinned to *stopping* by a live ``stopping_since``; or a
mid-wake ``waking``) is exactly what wake must rescue, so activate now
UNCONDITIONALLY clears both winding-down anchors — ``stopping_since`` AND
``waking_since`` → 0.0 — so the fresh ``desired_state="online"`` intent can never be
contradicted by a stale marker and the member reconciles cleanly back up.
post.summary: Activate: write desired_state=online intent (does NOT flip online).

post.description:
Deactivate a member (handover 層3): write the owner's INTENT
``desired_state="offline"`` and stamp ``stopping_since`` — a graceful STOP that
RETAINS the roster row (``status`` stays ``active``), in contrast to dismiss
(a soft delete that flips ``status="removed"``). A deactivated member can be
re-spawned later. Sets intent + shutdown signal ONLY — does NOT flip
``online`` (the warden tears the live session down and the presence endpoint
reports the winding-down phase back). ``stopping_since`` derives the
stopping/stopped presence; a ``member`` delta fans out immediately.

RBAC (control-others): route-table ``requires="admin_agent"`` — only an
owner-scoped token OR an admin-role (assistant) member may deactivate a
member; an ordinary agent → 403.
post.summary: Deactivate: desired_state=offline + stamp stopping_since (retains row).

post.description:
Force-stop a member: IMMEDIATELY kill the live session, bypassing BOTH the
120s graceful-stop grace clock AND the ~30s reconcile cadence.

This is the escalation the cockpit surfaces once a member is already *stopping*
(inside its graceful-stop grace): instead of waiting for the agent to self-stop
(up to 120s) or for the reconcile tick to eventually dispatch the robust stop,
the owner force-kills now. The handler writes the STOP intent
(``desired_state=offline``
+ stamps ``stopping_since`` if unset, so presence reads coherently) and then
dispatches the SINGLE robust STOP straight to the member's warden via
:func:`_dispatch_robust_stop_now` — the warden's ``stop()`` → ``escalateKill``
ladder performs the SIGKILL (tmux kill-session → force killpg the process group).

The grace clock lives in the reconcile machine's desired_state=offline arm (it waits
``stop_grace`` before emitting the robust stop); dispatching the stop directly
here skips that wait entirely. The reconcile tick remains the idempotent backstop
— a robust stop against an already-dead session is a no-op.

RBAC (control-others): route-table ``requires="admin_agent"`` — only an
owner-scoped token OR an admin-role (assistant) member may force-stop a
member; an ordinary agent → 403. A ``member`` delta
fans out on the intent write.
post.summary: Force-stop: robust STOP now, bypassing the graceful-stop grace.

post.description:
Refocus a member's context (§3.4 #14): record the owner's REFOCUS intent.
ONLY-ONLINE gate — refused 409 unless the member's DERIVED presence is online
(a context compaction is meaningless with no live session). On success the
server stamps ``refocus_since`` and fans a ``member`` delta; the ACTUAL
compaction is the warden/agent's job (it reads the marker and clears it).

RBAC (control-others): route-table ``requires="admin_agent"`` — only an
owner-scoped token OR an admin-role (assistant) member may refocus a
member; an ordinary agent → 403.
post.summary: Refocus a member's context (online-only, else 409).

post.description: Relocate a member to a machine (the owner cockpit's 改機器 for a roster member; the member twin of the outsource-worker relocate). Writes the owner-pinned ``desired_machine_id`` then runs the SAME event-driven reconcile the activate click uses (``reconcileMemberNow``): a LIVE member is auto-migrated onto the chosen machine (robust STOP the old session, the next tick re-spawns on the pin); an offline member just re-pins so the next wake lands there. PLACEMENT ONLY — unlike activate it NEVER touches ``desired_state`` (a relocate is not a wake). A ``member`` delta fans out immediately. A concrete (non-"", non-"auto") pin must name a real machine, else 404. P7c: ``member_id`` also accepts an outsource-worker id — an id naming no roster member falls through to the worker table and relocates that worker (the response is then an OutsourceWorkerDTO); an id in neither table stays the member 404.
post.summary: Relocate a member to a machine (placement only; never touches desired_state). Also accepts an outsource-worker id: the same move-one-agent verb relocates the worker.

get.description: List a member's webhook endpoints (M4 §1), oldest→newest. Each row carries the opaque token (owner-facing only) so the panel can render + copy the callback URL. 404 if the member is absent or soft-removed.

schema.title: Response Handle List Webhooks Api Members  Member Id  Webhooks Get

get.summary: List a member's webhook endpoints (WebhookEndpointDTO[]).

post.description: Create a webhook endpoint on a member (M4 §1). The server mints the opaque token and returns it on the DTO. A blank/duplicate/invalid endpoint_id is a 422/409; the member absent is a 404.
post.summary: Create a webhook endpoint (server mints the token).

delete.description: Permanently revoke a webhook endpoint (M4 §1) — the token is deleted and can never deliver again. 404 if the member or endpoint is absent.

patch.description: Edit a webhook endpoint (M4 §1): flip status (enable/disable — the revocation toggle, immediate) and/or edit purpose. endpoint_id is immutable. 404 if the member or endpoint is absent; a bad status is a 422.
patch.summary: Toggle status / edit purpose of a webhook endpoint.

get.description: The endpoint's /in debug ring buffer (M4 observability): the last 5 raw requests that resolved to this endpoint's token, newest first, each with its resolved outcome, JSON-serialised headers (<=4 KiB) and raw body text (<=16 KiB, `truncated` marks a cut). Owner-only (requires=owner - a plain agent or warden is a flat 403): these are raw UNVERIFIED external payloads, exposed for debugging a dead endpoint only. 404 if the member or endpoint is absent. Kept OFF WebhookEndpointDTO so the list wire stays light.

schema.title: Response Handle List Webhook Requests Api Members  Member Id  Webhooks  Endpoint Id  Requests Get

get.summary: Last 5 raw /in requests of one webhook endpoint (debug).

post.description:
Owner-gated mint of a long-lived AGENT token for an existing member.

Owner-only via the route table (``requires="owner"`` — the single
``authz.require_principal_class`` choke): any non-owner principal is a flat
403 before we mint. ``member_id`` must
resolve to a live roster member (else 404, via ``_resolve_member``). The owner
picks ``ttl_days``; the minted lifetime is capped at ``MAX_AGENT_TTL_SECS``
(400 days) so no token outlives the ceiling. The token is ``scope="agent"``
(NOT owner) with ``sub = member.id`` and ``exp = iat + ttl`` — additive to and
independent of the login / bootstrap mints, whose semantics are unchanged.
post.summary: Owner-gated mint of a long-lived agent JWT for a member (TTL capped).

get.description:
Monitoring telemetry (§3.8): live sessions + machines from the REAL roster
+ context gauge, FOLDED with the warden telemetry push (``app.state.telemetry``
— hardware, rate-limit pacing, per-session tokens/cost). NEVER fabricates a
number — where no source has reported, the field stays honest-None / the
accounts list stays honest-empty (量不到 = None, never 0).

Fold rules (ported from vibe-clicking's monitoring_get): a session's cost /
tokens come from ITS telemetry entry; machine hardware is the FRESHEST report
per host (CPU/RAM are point-in-time, NEVER summed); an account row carries the
5h/7d pacing from that account's freshest ``rate_limits`` and the SUM of its
sessions' cost.
get.summary: Monitoring telemetry (roster + context + warden push; honest — else).

post.description:
Ingest one warden telemetry report (§3.8) — the source-of-truth push that
fills the monitoring surface (``GET /api/monitoring``). Stored purely
IN-MEMORY on ``app.state.telemetry`` keyed by ``agent_id`` (no DB, ephemeral —
a fresh, honest-empty store on restart is fail-safe), MERGED onto the prior
entry so a partial report (hardware-only, say) never clobbers a sibling field.

Body carries the account-wide ``rate_limits`` (5h/7d), this session's own
``tokens``, the host ``hardware`` snapshot, the cumulative ``cost``, and the
``machine`` / ``account`` merge tags. A bad body (empty ``agent_id``, or no
telemetry field at all, or a wrong-typed field) is a flat 400 (NOT a 422).

IDENTITY FROM TOKEN (mirrors the context gauge): the telemetry key is the CALLER
(``current_actor`` — the verified JWT ``sub``), NEVER a self-reported ``agent_id``
(that param was removed) — so an agent can only ever report its OWN telemetry.
NOTE: ``command_result.member_id`` inside the body is a legit TARGET (the member a
warden executed against) and is UNTOUCHED — it is not the caller's self-identity.
post.summary: Ingest warden telemetry (hardware/limits/tokens/cost/self_update).

get.description: List live (not-yet-released) outsource workers with their bound task's title/status — the 外包 panel's data source (SPEC §4.1). Released workers drop off (their rows and chat history persist).

schema.title: Response Handle List Outsource Workers Api Outsource Workers Get

get.summary: List live outsource workers (codename, model, effort, task).

get.description: Read ONE outsource worker by id (its JWT sub) — the same projection the list serves, for the detail panel's post-relocate refresh (T-f190). 404 when the worker id is unknown.
get.summary: Read one outsource worker by id (detail-panel refresh).

get.description: Read the outsource worker's boot-context PREVIEW (T-ba6b): the server re-assembles the persona/boot text with the SAME fold the spawn path uses (buildWorkerBootContext — worker_context.md seed + 你的身分 + the bound task in full + the type manual Q1/Q2/Q3 + learnings) from the CURRENT DB rows, WITHOUT minting any token. Owner-only cockpit read (the text embeds the full task + manual). 404 for an unknown worker or a worker whose bound task is gone. HONEST caveat the UI must carry: this is today's re-assembly, not a verbatim spawn-time record — nothing is stored (no prompt column, no migration).
get.summary: Read an outsource worker's boot-context preview (owner-only).

post.description: Change (換 model) an outsource worker's model + effort (T-f190 lifecycle), the worker twin of the member model/effort edit. Persists the new model (blank ⇒ launcher default) and effort; when the worker is ACTIVE + online it kills+respawns so the new model takes effect NOW, otherwise (assigned/stopped) it only persists — the next spawn/restart bakes it in. 404 unknown/released. The owner mental model: an outsource worker is just a member the system creates and deletes, so it reuses the SAME lifecycle mechanisms. Owner-only (route requires=owner), MCP-excluded (workers are server/owner-managed; agents never drive these).
post.summary: Change (換 model) an outsource worker's model/effort (owner-only).

post.description: Refocus (換手) an outsource worker (T-32e1): the worker twin of refocus_member. Stamps refocus_since, then kills the current session and re-spawns it so a FRESH worker picks the SAME bound task back up from its task plan / step notes (a worker has no member fold — continuity lives in the task). ONLINE-ONLY: 409 unless the worker is active with a live session; 409 for a stopped worker (restart first); 404 unknown/released. The owner mental model: an outsource worker is just a member the system creates and deletes, so it reuses the SAME lifecycle mechanisms. Owner-only (route requires=owner), MCP-excluded (workers are server/owner-managed; agents never drive these).
post.summary: Refocus (換手) an outsource worker's context (owner-only, online-only else 409).

post.description: Relocate an outsource worker to a machine (T-f190): the owner cockpit's 改機器 operation, the worker twin of member activate's machine bind. Writes desired_machine_id, kills the current session, and clears pacing so the next scheduler tick re-spawns on the chosen machine — the same 殺舊 session + 清 pacing + 讓下一 tick 重生 semantics the shared-FSM zombie-takeover uses, WITHOUT touching lifecycle (the worker stays assigned/active). Admin-gated since P7c (requires=admin_agent, the exact member relocate floor).
post.summary: Relocate an outsource worker to a machine (admin-gated).

post.description: Restart (重啟) a stopped outsource worker (T-f190 lifecycle), the inverse of stop: set desired_state back to 'online' and re-dispatch (重啟 = 再 dispatch, a fresh worker_start onto the pinned/preferred machine). 409 when the worker is not stopped (desired_state != offline) (nothing to restart); 404 unknown/released. The owner mental model: an outsource worker is just a member the system creates and deletes, so it reuses the SAME lifecycle mechanisms. Owner-only (route requires=owner), MCP-excluded (workers are server/owner-managed; agents never drive these).
post.summary: Restart (重啟) a stopped outsource worker (owner-only; 409 if not stopped).

post.description: Stop (停止) an outsource worker (T-f190 lifecycle): set desired_state='offline' (a direct mirror of member.desired_state — which makes every scheduler auto-revival path skip it: the shared reconcile-FSM rescue never revives an owner-held-down worker), clear any in-flight refocus, and kill the session WITHOUT re-dispatching. The worker projects presence 'stopping'/'stopped' (honest, never fake-green); the bound task stays in its own status. Idempotent. 404 unknown/released. The owner mental model: an outsource worker is just a member the system creates and deletes, so it reuses the SAME lifecycle mechanisms. Owner-only (route requires=owner), MCP-excluded (workers are server/owner-managed; agents never drive these).
post.summary: Stop (停止) an outsource worker (owner-only; kill + hold down).

get.description:
Owner-gated explicit update check (檢查更新): synchronously asks GitHub
Releases (pkyosx/OffiCraft, anonymous) for the newest admissible release
and compares it against the running version. Failure to reach GitHub
degrades gracefully to status "unknown" — never a 5xx.
get.summary: Check GitHub Releases for a newer official OffiCraft version.

get.description:
The reply-card list — LIGHT rows (``list_reply_cards`` MCP tool; owner ruling:
卡只需要 title+決策). ``?status=waiting`` (the default) returns cards still
waiting on the owner, longest-waiting first; ``?status=answered`` returns cards
answered within the last 24h, newest answer first; ``?status=expired`` returns
cards the owner marked expired within the last 24h (keyed off ``expired_ts``),
newest first. Each row carries the card's
``summary`` (its title) and — on an answered row — the decision DIGEST
(picked option index + original wording, an answer-text preview, the
attachment count), NEVER the ``body`` or the full ``options`` text; pull one
card in full with ``get_reply_card``. ``?limit=N`` (N > 0) caps the rows AFTER
the pane's ordering (the pane's first N survive); absent / non-positive = the
whole pane.

schema.title: Response Handle List Reply Cards Api Reply Cards Get

get.summary: List reply cards — LIGHT rows (?status=waiting|answered|expired; ?limit= caps; get_reply_card for full).

post.description: Open a reply card (agent-side; the MCP tool ``create_reply_card``). The initiator is ALWAYS the verified JWT ``sub``. Server validation: ``kind`` must be ``decision``|``action``; ``summary`` must be non-blank; ``options`` must be 1..4 non-blank strings (index 0 = the AI recommendation) — violations are a 400. Opening a card ALSO posts one chat message (initiator → owner, body = summary, ``meta.reply_card_id`` = the card id) so the ask rides the normal chat stream (unread red dot, permanent history); the card's ``chat_message_id`` links back to it. AUTO task/step binding: when the initiator is the executor of EXACTLY ONE active task (``in_progress``|``waiting_owner``), the card binds to that task, and to its CURRENT step (the single ``in_progress`` step, else the single ``waiting_owner`` step) when that is unambiguous — the bound step enters ``waiting_owner`` carrying the card (``reply_card_id``), and the task follows into ``waiting_owner`` unless the step sits in a parallel group (sibling lanes may still run). An initiator with NO active task opens a plain unbound 請示. An initiator that IS executing active work but cannot be bound to both levels (2+ active tasks, or no single current step) is REFUSED with 409 and no card is minted — a card bound to a task but to no step places no ``waiting_owner`` hold, so the task would run on to ``done`` underneath an ask the owner can then never answer (T-4166). The explicit exit is ``open_gate`` (POST /api/tasks/{task_id}/steps/{step_id}/gate), which names the step. No request field selects the binding. Fans one ``chat`` and one ``reply_card`` SSE delta (plus a ``task`` delta when a step was bound).
post.summary: Open a reply card: an ask the owner must answer (options ≤4, [0]=AI pick).

get.description: The cockpit navigation badge count: reply cards with status ``waiting``. Kept as its own endpoint so the badge can poll/refetch cheaply on ``reply_card`` SSE deltas without pulling the card list.
get.summary: Waiting reply-card count (the cockpit badge).

get.description: Read one reply card in full — summary, body, options (original wording), status, the linked chat message id, and the owner's answer when answered. This is the agent's pull path after a ``reply_card`` SSE delta: the delta says 'this card changed', this read carries the full card context + the answer back to the agent. Unknown id → 404.
get.summary: Read one reply card (full context: options, status, answer).

post.description: Answer a WAITING reply card — the only POSITIVE close (there is deliberately no close/skip surface; the owner-only expire action is the sole other exit and is NOT an answer). The answer is an ``option_idx`` and/or free ``text`` (+ attachments); an empty answer is 400, an out-of-range ``option_idx`` is 400. Any real answer — including a counter-question typed as text — flips the card to ``answered`` and stamps ``answered_ts``; if the reply did not settle the original question the agent opens a NEW card (never reopens this one). Answering an already-answered card is a 409 (revise via PUT instead); an expired card is a 409 too (terminal — if the question still matters the agent opens a NEW card). When the card is bound to a task step (open_gate / create_reply_card auto-bind), the FIRST answer also releases the waiting_owner hold — the server restores the step (and the task, once its last bound card is answered) to in_progress; the agent then advances the work itself. Fans one ``reply_card`` SSE delta (badge −1; the agent's own connection receives it and refetches the card for the full answer context).
post.summary: Answer a waiting reply card — the only way a card closes.

put.description: Revise an ANSWERED card's answer (重新決定 — the owner changing their OWN answer; distinct from the agent asking again via a new card). Same body + validation as POST; the card MUST already be answered (a waiting card is a 409 — answer it with POST). The answer is replaced wholesale, ``answered_ts`` re-stamps (the card re-enters the 24h recently-answered window), and status STAYS ``answered`` — a revision never reopens the card or re-counts the badge. Fans one ``reply_card`` SSE delta so the agent picks up the updated decision.
put.summary: Revise an answered card's answer (重新決定): stays answered.

post.description: Mark a WAITING reply card EXPIRED (標為過期) — the owner-only terminal exit that is NOT an answer: the ask went stale (懸太久、答案已不可靠) or its task is already closed, and the owner declines to answer it. The card flips to ``expired`` and stamps ``expired_ts``; there is no request body, no undo, no reopen (if the question still matters the agent opens a NEW card with current context). When the card is bound to a task step, expiring releases the waiting_owner hold exactly like a first answer — the server restores the step (and the task, once its last bound card is settled) to in_progress; a card orphaned on an already-terminal task may still be expired (its ONLY exit — answering an orphan is 409) and the closed task is left untouched. An already-answered or already-expired card is a 409. Fans one ``reply_card`` SSE delta (status=expired; the initiating agent's connection receives it and refetches the card).
post.summary: Mark a waiting card expired (owner-only; not an answer; terminal).

get.description:
A BOUNDED, deterministic wake snapshot for the caller (``resume_summary`` MCP
tool, zero params; ``GET /api/resume-summary``).

Identity-locked to the caller's VERIFIED JWT ``sub`` (``actor``): assembles ONE
bounded block so a waking / handed-over agent resumes without paging every plane
by hand. It surfaces the caller's recent chat (the most recent ``RESUME_CHAT_N``
messages where sender == caller OR recipient == caller, oldest→newest, each body
truncated to ``RESUME_CHAT_BODY_PREVIEW``), the caller's OPEN tasks as LIGHT rows
(executor == caller, non-terminal, most recently updated first, capped to
``RESUME_TASKS_N`` — owner ruling: 任務不該包含細節; each row names the task, its
status/priority, the current node NAME and progress, plus ``detail_chars`` — the
size of the plan text it omits; NO steps / DoD ride the snapshot, ``get_task``
pulls them per task), an ``overview`` size/概要 block (counts + character sizes,
the caller's reply-card counts included — peek it FIRST, then decide what to pull
and whether to digest via a sub-agent), the caller's ``identity`` and a fixed
bounded-snapshot ``note``. DETERMINISTIC and read-only; a caller with no chat and
no tasks gets EMPTY lists, never an error.
get.summary: Bounded LIGHT wake snapshot for the caller (recent chat + light task rows + size overview).

get.description:
The size-only PEEK of the wake snapshot (``peek_resume_summary_size`` MCP
tool, zero params; ``GET /api/resume-summary-size``) — step ONE of the two-step
boot.

Identity-locked to the caller's VERIFIED JWT ``sub`` (``actor``): returns the
SAME ``overview`` counts/sizes a full ``resume_summary`` would report (both go
through one shared server assembly, so the reported sizes cannot drift from what
resume_summary actually carries) plus ``estimated_total_chars`` — a derived
single number (the snapshot's chat body chars plus the plan text its task rows
omit) to gate the boot decision on — and a fixed guidance ``note``. It carries
NO chat bodies and NO task rows of any kind: peeking it costs a few hundred
bytes, so a waking agent sizes ``resume_summary`` BEFORE deciding whether to
pull it into its own context or hand the pull to a cheap sub-agent (e.g. haiku)
that returns a compressed digest. DETERMINISTIC and read-only; a caller with no
chat and no tasks gets zeroes, never an error.
get.summary: Size-only PEEK of the wake snapshot (overview counts/sizes + estimated_total_chars, NO content).

get.description:
List every role definition (§3.4 #23): the seed roles (folded with any
owner edit) FIRST, then every owner-created CUSTOM role (M2-2 — an overlay
with no file seed, non-tombstoned). Custom roles are appended after the
seeds in stable roster order, so the settings list reads seed → custom.

schema.title: Response Handle List Roles Api Roles Get

get.summary: List role definitions (seed defaults + owner edits).

post.description:
Create ONE custom role + its ONE founding member (M2-2 角色誌新增;
``POST /api/roles``). One pair per call — 同角色多成員 / 零成員 is out of
scope by decree. Admin-gated (requires="admin_agent") like every role write.

The server mints BOTH ids (role key ``r-<hex12>``, member id ``m-<hex12>``)
— never client-supplied. The role's ``definition_md`` starts from the fixed
TEMPLATE (兩 section 待填說明: 「你是誰」/「你做什麼」) so the owner edits a
scaffold, not a blank page. The member starts OFFLINE + active (creating
never spawns a runtime — presence is the SSE projection and there is no
connection); it surfaces on the office roster immediately via the ``member``
delta ``put_member`` fans. ``member_name`` is OPTIONAL: omitted/blank ⇒ the
server picks a fresh Mira-style name from ``domain.member.MEMBER_NAME_POOL``,
skipping every name already on the roster (case-insensitive, removed rows
included so a revived audit-trail name never doubles). ``model`` is a free
launch string (blank/omitted ⇒ CLI default); ``effort`` is the closed
low/medium/high vocabulary (422 outside it; omitted ⇒ medium) — both are
baked into the NEXT wake's launch command by the reconcile START payload
(change-takes-effect-on-next-wake).
post.summary: Create a custom role + its founding member (one pair per call).

delete.description:
HARD-delete a CUSTOM role + everything it owns (M2-2 刪除角色;
``DELETE /api/roles/{role}``). Admin-gated (requires="admin_agent"). Three
server-side防線 (never UI-only):

  1. ONLY a custom role is deletable — a seed role (出廠助理, or any
     role_key with a file seed) is a flat 403, so core/seed members (Mira)
     can never be deleted through this seam. Unknown role → 404.
  2. NO member of the role may be ONLINE — online is the hub's live SSE
     projection (the SAME source ``handle_list_members`` reads), and any
     online member → 409 (the FE surfaces 「有成員在線上,無法刪除」).
  3. The delete is a COMPLETE HARD cascade (not the status="removed"
     soft-remove — that stays the dismiss seam): every member of the role
     (including previously soft-removed ones) is physically deleted, along
     with its conversations (chat messages + their attachment blobs), its
     read receipts, the role's lessons overlays, its in-memory telemetry /
     context-gauge entries, and finally the role overlay itself.

Categories that do NOT exist in this office (no store, nothing to clear):
等我回覆卡 (pending/clicking), handoff records, and durable per-session rows
(the member session fields were dropped by migration 0024; live session
state is the SSE connection itself, refused while online by 防線 2).
delete.summary: Hard-delete a custom role + its members (seed → 403; online → 409).

get.description: Read one folded role definition (§3.4 #24). Unknown role → 404.
get.summary: Read one role definition (unknown → 404).

post.description:
Edit a role definition (§3.4 #25): ``{name?, definition_md?}``. Unknown role
→ 404. The overlay is SELF-CONTAINED, so we fold the current effective doc,
apply the supplied fields on top, and store the full result (``is_default`` →
False). ``name`` applies ONLY to a CUSTOM role — a SEED role is name-locked
and IGNORES a supplied ``name`` (ignore-not-reject, per the §3.4 #25
contract); the renamed title then feeds every ``role_name`` fold (roster
cards, member DTOs) since the custom role's own name is that single truth.
post.summary: Edit a role definition ({name?, definition_md?}; locked names skip).

post.description:
Reset a role definition to seed (§3.4 #26): idempotent tombstone of the
overlay. Unknown SEED role → 404 (a seed role must exist to reset to).
post.summary: Reset a role definition to seed (idempotent tombstone overlay).

post.description:
``restart_self()`` — the agent's SELF-TRIGGERED recycle (identity from token, NO
member_id). A self-op: by construction it can only ever restart the CALLER, so it
is strictly WEAKER than ``refocus_member`` (which is admin-gated and targets any
member) — zero privilege-escalation surface.

EFFECT: stamps the CALLER's ``refocus_since`` and fans a ``member`` delta, exactly
like the owner's refocus. Nothing else is dispatched here — the STANDARD recycle
orchestration (§4.5) then carries the handover: the delta reaches the agent's OWN
listen connection → the RecycleHook prints the five-step handover SOP → the agent
runs it (report_stopping → baton → lessons → report_stopped) → the server kills →
respawns IN PLACE. Same already-tested machinery as an owner refocus, just
self-triggered; the agent never mints a token or kills its own process.

ABUSE GUARDS (two, both refuse LOUDLY so the agent can read them):
  * ONLINE-ONLY (409) — a self-restart is meaningless with no live session; the
    caller's DERIVED presence must be online, mirroring ``refocus_member``.
  * MINIMUM-LIVENESS (429) — a call within the first 10 minutes after the session
    connected (server-authoritative ``boot_ts`` from the SSE first-connect edge) is
    refused, so a freshly respawned agent cannot immediately self-restart again and
    spin a respawn storm. A missing boot_ts (server-restart amnesia) FAILS OPEN —
    never a false 429 on a long-lived session.

``reason`` (optional): a short human note for WHY — recorded on the recycle log
line so an operator can distinguish a self-restart from an owner refocus.
post.summary: restart_self(): self-triggered recycle (online-only 409; min-liveness 429).

post.description:
``report_stopped()`` — shutdown-done report (identity from token, NO member_id).

Stamps the CALLER's ``stopped_since`` ONCE (anchor semantics — never re-stamped
while the agent keeps holding its SSE and re-reports) so the server reconcile
machine can tell a refocus-marked, still-online agent has FINISHED its graceful
dump and is ready to be robust-stopped for respawn. This is the PERSISTENT,
cross-cadence discriminator of "暫斷 (a ~1s EOF reconnect) vs dump 完": a reconnect
never reaches this report, so it never stamps and never triggers a recycle kill.
``stopping_since`` is retained (>0) so the derived presence is *stopped* once the
SSE drops; ``refocus_since`` is DELIBERATELY left set (the dump-done marker stays
in play until the machine kills → respawn → ``report_waking`` clears both markers).

EVENT-DRIVEN RECYCLE KILL (phase ②): on the FIRST stopped report (the anchor
transition), if a recycle is pending (still ``desired_state==online`` +
refocus-marked)
fire the robust STOP at the warden RIGHT NOW — Seth's event-driven kill — instead
of waiting up to ~30s for the reconcile cadence to observe it. Gated on the anchor
so repeated stopped re-reports do NOT re-dispatch. The machine reconcile recycle
branch stays the idempotent BACKSTOP.
post.summary: report_stopped(): anchor the caller's stopped; fire recycle kill.

post.description:
``report_stopping()`` — graceful-shutdown-in-progress report (identity from
token, NO member_id). Stamps the CALLER's ``stopping_since`` IF UNSET → derives
*stopping* while its SSE is still live, *stopped* once it drops.

``waking_since`` is intentionally NOT cleared (a waking→stopping mid-startup abort
is pathological; ``stopping_since`` dominates the projection). ``online`` is a pure
SSE projection, never written here.
post.summary: report_stopping(): stamp the caller's stopping_since (graceful stop).

get.description: The outsource worker's claim (GET /api/self/task, identity-locked): the task bound to the caller's JWT sub plus the type's manual snapshot (SOP + learnings; null for an ad-hoc task). The FIRST claim flips the worker assigned → active. A caller with no bound worker row (any roster member included) is a 404.
get.summary: Outsource worker's claim: read the task bound to the caller.

post.description:
``report_waking()`` — the boot report (identity from token, NO member_id).

Stamps the CALLER's ``waking_since`` → derives *waking* until the SSE connects
(⇒ online) or the wall-clock TTL lapses. ``online`` is untouched (pure SSE
projection).

RECYCLE LOOP BREAK: a fresh session's FIRST report clears ALL the recycle markers
— ``refocus_since`` / ``stopped_since`` / ``stopping_since`` → 0.0 — so the
respawned agent sheds the refocus intent, the dump-done stamp AND the graceful-
shutdown anchor BEFORE it goes online. The boot序 guarantees this report lands
before the SSE connects, so the new session can never inherit a stale marker and
re-trigger a recycle (or derive *stopping*) on itself.

``model`` (optional): when present the agent self-reports the REAL model it was
launched with (from OC_MODEL) — only the running agent knows this — so the server
writes the true model onto the member. Omitted ⇒ untouched (PATCH semantics).
post.summary: report_waking(): stamp the caller's waking + clear recycle markers.

get.description:
Read the owner-adjustable settings (owner-gated): the login TTL, the context
auto-handover threshold, and the read-only self-healed port (null while the
server runs on its preferred port).
get.summary: Read the owner-adjustable settings.

patch.description:
Partially update the owner-adjustable settings (owner-gated). Only supplied
fields change; a change is durable (DB) AND live immediately — `token_ttl`
applies from the next login, `handover_pct` applies from the next context
report. `token_ttl` outside the 12h/24h/7d/30d whitelist or `handover_pct`
outside 40..90 is a 422 (nothing is written).
patch.summary: Edit settings (login TTL / handover threshold); live immediately.

get.description: List task manuals (the 設定 › 任務手冊 cards).

schema.title: Response Handle List Task Manuals Api Task Manuals Get

get.summary: List task types (match by display_name/purpose; address by type_key).

post.description: Create one task type (a blank manual). Pass ``display_name``; the server mints the ``tm-`` type_key id and returns it in the DTO. Legacy: an explicit ``type_key`` is still accepted verbatim as the id (duplicate → 409; blank display_name backfills to it). Agent floor: any agent may create a manual; ``assignee`` is OWNER-ONLY governance — a non-owner supplying it is a 403.
post.summary: Create a task type: pass display_name; the server mints and returns the tm- type_key id (legacy explicit type_key still accepted; duplicate → 409; assignee = owner-only).

delete.description: Delete a task type. Refused (409) while NON-terminal tasks of the type exist (SPEC §5.1); closed tasks never block.
delete.summary: Delete a task type (open tasks of the type → 409).

get.description: Read one manual in full (purpose / fields / SOP / learnings / assignee) — the intake's type-judgement AND the planner's blueprint read. Unknown type → 404.
get.summary: Read one task manual (purpose/fields/SOP/learnings/assignee).

post.description: Partial manual edit — only supplied fields change. Agent floor: content fields (purpose / fields / sop_md / learnings) are agent-editable; ``assignee`` is OWNER-ONLY governance — a non-owner supplying it is a 403. ``assignee`` is {"kind":"member","member_id":…} or {"kind":"outsource","model":…,"effort":…,"copies":N}; {} unsets it. Unknown keys are REJECTED (422) rather than silently dropped: this endpoint writes the same learnings document as ``write_task_learnings``, which spells the field ``text`` instead of ``learnings`` — sending the wrong name used to answer 200 having written nothing (T-2d99). Omitting a field remains legal; only unrecognised names are refused.
post.summary: Edit a task manual (partial; owner's structure edit).

post.description: Whole-doc replace of a type's learnings (MCP ``write_task_learnings`` — the agent's task-close write-back; the replace_lessons shape). Unknown type → 404.
post.summary: Whole-doc replace of a type's learnings (task-close write-back).

post.description:
Anchor-addressed PATCH of a type's learnings (MCP ``patch_task_learnings`` — the learnings twin of ``patch_lessons``).
